sections.paper_id: Adaryukov et al. - 2025 - Worth the weight An examination of unstructured and structured data in graduate admissions.
paragraphs: Worth the Weight: An Examination of Unstructured and Structured Data in Graduate Admissions Imagine you are a member of a graduate admissions committee considering the application of one Gil Nightray. You have never met Gil, but you can access their entire application: three recommendation letters from their professors, one personal statement, Graduate Record Examination (GRE) scores, their college grade point average (GPA), and the demographic information they've provided. Your task is to recommend whether Gil should be admitted to or denied from a prospective graduate program. How will you decide? Will you focus entirely on their grades and standardized test scores? Will you give weight to what their letter writers say about them, and if so, which parts?

sections.paper_id: Adaryukov et al. - 2025 - Worth the weight An examination of unstructured and structured data in graduate admissions.
paragraphs: The Gil Nightray application is one of many examples of multiattribute choice-a choice requiring the evaluation of two or more options across two or more attributes to select the more desirable option (Keeney & Raiffa, 1993). These types of choices are common in everyday life, ranging from the relatively mundane (e.g., choosing what snack to eat this afternoon) to the highly consequential (e.g., diagnosing a clinical patient).

Thus, the questions of how people should and do make such choices are of great scientific and practical interest (Gigerenzer & Gaissmeier, 2011;Keeney & Raiffa, 1993;Oppenheimer & Kelso, 2015;Weber & Johnson, 2009).

The typical approach to studying multiattribute choice is to conceptualize each option as composed of a fixed number of common attributes. Each attribute, in turn, is represented with a precise numerical variable (e.g., price), dichotomous variable (e.g., presence or absence), or categorical variable (e.g., year in program). We refer to these attributes as structured data, adopting the definition from data science, which uses the term to describe data-usually quantitative-that is highly organized and decipherable by machine learning algorithms (IBM Cloud Education, 2021). In the case of multiattribute choice, structured data facilitates the use of multiattribute utility theory to identify the normative solution for how people should decide, i.e., by choosing the option that maximizes their utility (Keeney & Raiffa, 1993;Von Winterfeldt & Edwards, 1986). This structured approach also facilitates the use of experimental and computational approaches to study how people decide (Gigerenzer et al., 1999;Payne et al., 1993;Wedell, 2015). For instance, in experimental work, choice options and their attributes are displayed to the participants in an attribute-by-option matrix, allowing researchers to observe which options people consider, which attributes of those options they consider, and in what order they attend to those attributes when making a choice. (e.g., Bröder, 2003;Bröder & Schiffer, 2003;Payne et al., 1993;Svenson, 1979).

While many multiattribute choices are made with only structured data, arguably, a much larger class of multiattribute choices must be made with consideration of unstructured data. In data science, this term refers to data that can not be stored in a traditional row-column database and are typically qualitative in nature (IBM Cloud Education, 2021). In the case of multiattribute choice, this type of attribute information arises when someone has to, for example, choose a movie or meal from the name alone.

Because no attribute values are explicitly provided, people must make decisions using the information they recall from memory (Bhatia & Stewart, 2018;Bröder & Schiffer, 2003), from the features they perceive (Krajbich et al., 2010), or from what they learn from experience (Liu & Trueblood, 2023). In other cases, the unstructured attribute information is contained within text, conversations, images, video, audio, etc. Perhaps the most prominent example of a multiattribute choice that relies on unstructured attribute information is in the domain of competitive selection, e.g., occupational hiring and student admissions. While structured, quantitative measures are often important in selection, the people making such decisions often base their judgments on unstructured documents as well (e.g., essays, letters of recommendation, interviews, and resumés; Ginkel et al., 2010;Max et al., 2010;Speer, 2018). There is a growing body of research on how to use machine learning methods such as natural language processing to analyze and integrate unstructured data into selection procedures (e.g., Putka et al., 2018;Guo et al., 2021). The general question we take on in this paper is: can we use natural language processing methods to understand how people make decisions with such data?

sections.paper_id: Adaryukov et al. - 2025 - Worth the weight An examination of unstructured and structured data in graduate admissions.
paragraphs: More specifically, we seek to deepen scientific understanding of how people integrate unstructured, qualitative data with structured, quantitative data when making real-world decisions (Shiffrin, 2021). To do so, we draw from a unique dataset of applications to the University of Kansas (n = 2, 231), for admission to graduate school in the Fall semester of 2013. The applications were submitted to 37 university departments, including science, technology, engineering, mathematics (STEM), the social sciences, and the humanities. For each applicant, the dataset contained structured and unstructured data: their GRE scores, undergraduate GPA, letters of recommendation, personal statement, and demographic data, including race, ethnicity, gender, and nationality. The dataset also included the final admissions decision for each applicant (i.e., whether they were admitted to the program).

We do not have formal reports on how the different departments made acceptance decisions, but our informal knowledge is that each department used a holistic, non-mechanical method to make each decision. Generally, in a holistic admissions decision, a broad range of characteristics, including test scores, grades, prior experience, letters of recommendation, and characteristics inferred from personal statements, are considered (Kent & McCarthy, 2016). These evaluations are based on individual judgment and group consensus, with the key characteristic being reliance on human judgment instead of an algorithm or formula to reach a decision (Kuncel et al., 2013). Thus, the dataset allowed us to examine a real-world, high-stakes decision that relied on human judgment to evaluate and integrate structured and unstructured data.

Graduate admissions is a unique context to study how people make multiattribute choices. Prior research implies admissions decisions can, to some degree, be predicted from the quantitative attributes of the student's application, particularly their GRE and undergraduate GPA (Dawes, 1971). 1 The use of undergraduate GPA and GRE scores can be justified as these variables predict applicants' future performance along multiple metrics (e.g., average graduate GPA, first-year graduate GPA, comprehensive examination scores, publication and citation counts, and faculty ratings) across departments and years (Kuncel et al., 2001). However, the prediction from quantitative information is far from perfect, implying other information, such as letters of recommendation and personal statements, may be used to make the decision. The push to use more holistic admissions implies even more emphasis on non-cognitive and personal attributes gleaned from prior work experience, letters of recommendation, and characteristics inferred from personal statements (Kent & McCarthy, 2016). There is little work on how this structured and unstructured information is evaluated and ultimately used to make holistic decisions. Thus, our goal of using natural language processing to model how unstructured and structured information is integrated can help address an open question in the academic domain.

A second reason that graduate admissions decisions are of interest is that there is considerable concern about the degree to which an applicant's demographic characteristics (e.g., gender, race, and international status) are considered when deciding to admit an applicant to graduate school. Gender stereotypes associating women with lesser competence (especially in many STEM fields) may lower their perceived fit to some programs (e.g., see Moss-Racusin et al., 2012;Madera et al., 2019, but cf. Ceci et al., 2023), and gender differences in the content and evaluation of letters of recommendation may lead to devaluation of women's skills (Trix & Psenkax, 2003;Kaatz et al., 2015, but cf. Niessen & Neumann, 2022). Moreover, concerns have been raised that GRE scores may disproportionately disfavor minority and international students due to unequal access to 1 Dawes (1971) also included a measure of the quality of the undergraduate institution. This measure, GRE, and GPA had a combined multiple correlation of .78 with graduate admissions committee ratings.
preparation resources (Gómez et al., 2021;De Los Reyes & Uddin, 2021) but cf Xie, 2013;

Sanchez & Moore, 2019, regarding limited effects of test prep in other contexts.. Still less clear is how demographic information like gender, race, and ethnicity are associated with graduate admissions decisions once other information within an application, including structured data and prior experience, is accounted for. Many academic departments have struggled to recruit and retain minority applicants, and admissions remain a major hurdle for prospective students to clear. Understanding the use of applicant information in admissions decisions thus has immediate practical, ethical, and social justice implications (Callahan et al., 2018;Trix & Psenkax, 2003).

sections.paper_id: Adaryukov et al. - 2025 - Worth the weight An examination of unstructured and structured data in graduate admissions.
paragraphs: The first step in addressing our questions is modeling (and quantifying) unstructured attribute data so that it can be incorporated into decision models. To model how graduate admissions committees use unstructured attribute data (e.g., the text in letters of recommendation and personal statements), we drew on topic modeling methodologies (Blei et al., 2003;Blei & Lafferty, 2007;Griffiths et al., 2007). In topic modeling, one establishes a probability distribution over a set of topics. This distribution determines the overall meaning of the document. Then, for each word in the document, the author chooses a topic randomly based on the topic distribution and then draws a word from the distribution over words for that topic (Steyvers & Griffiths, 2007). Based on this generative model, knowing the co-occurrence of words in a corpus, a topic model uses statistical inference to estimate the distribution of words making up a topic and the distribution of topics making up a document. Such an approach means that topics are extracted from the unstructured data computationally rather than being determined by the researcher a priori. For instance, if a document contained the words "theater" and "reel"nearby, a topic model would infer that said document was likely to contain a topic aligned with film studies. Each topic is modeled as a probability distribution over the words it can generate, with higher probability words representing the topic's meaning. For instance, the aforementioned film studies topic would have a high probability of generating such words as "theater", "reel", "camera", and "scene". Modeling topics as probability distributions of words allows models to account for more nuanced and contextual definitions of words rather than locking them into a single context. Returning to our earlier example, the word "reel" might have a high probability of being generated by topics related to "movies" and "fishing", depending on what other words were found in documents containing it (Finch et al., 2018).

In addition to our questions about unstructured data, we want to ask about its relationship with applicant race and gender. To address this question and others, we go a step beyond conventional topic modeling and use structural topic models (STM; Blei & Lafferty, 2007;M. E. Roberts et al., 2014;Wang et al., 2011). STMs account for correlations among topics rather than assuming they are independent (Blei & Lafferty, 2007) and factor in associations with outside covariates (M. E. Roberts et al., 2019). In other words, rather than assuming the topic distribution for a document is independent of another document, STMs help capture possible associations between documents via key covariates like the race or gender of an applicant. Similarly, STMs also allow for dependence between the word distributions for topics captured by the same or a different set of covariates. Thus, STM techniques allow for even more nuanced analyses of the relationships among topics and analyses of the relationships between topics and outside context.

Once we extracted topics from our text corpora (i.e., letters of recommendation and personal statements -note that these corpora were modeled separately), we drew on a method called policy capture (Cooksey, 1996) to analyze how the information in the application package was used to decide to accept or reject each applicant. Traditionally, policy capture has evaluated cue usage using regression-based methods, with variance captured by individual predictors representing the weight those cues had on the decision process (e.g., Dawes, 1971;Spence & Keeping, 2009;Dahling & Thompson, 2010). of these topics has a probability of generating words that appear in the text. From the probability that a topic generated a word, one can calculate the overall probability that said topic produced a given document.

Ordinarily, unstructured data is difficult for regression to accommodate and requires specialized techniques (Putka et al., 2022). This has caused issues when adapting policy capture to more naturalistic contexts, where structured cues are often more difficult to come by (Ebbesen & Konecni, 1980;Shiffrin, 2021). By deriving the structure of our documents through topic modeling, however, we can adapt the latent meaning within them into a quantitative, numerical form (i.e., the topic proportions for each document). This approach allows us to explore the question: when provided both structured and unstructured data, how did decision makers integrate them?

Based on this generative model, knowing the co-occurrence of words in a corpus, a topic model uses statistical inference to estimate the distribution of words making up a topic and the distribution of topics making up a document. Such an approach means that topics are extracted from the unstructured data computationally rather than being determined by the researcher a priori. For instance, if a document contained the words "theater" and "reel"nearby, a topic model would infer that said document was likely to contain a topic aligned with film studies. Each topic is modeled as a probability distribution over the words it can generate, with higher probability words representing the topic's meaning. For instance, the aforementioned film studies topic would have a high probability of generating such words as "theater", "reel", "camera", and "scene". Modeling topics as probability distributions of words allows models to account for more nuanced and contextual definitions of words rather than locking them into a single context. Returning to our earlier example, the word "reel" might have a high probability of being generated by topics related to "movies" and "fishing", depending on what other words were found in documents containing it (Finch et al., 2018).

In addition to our questions about unstructured data, we want to ask about its relationship with applicant race and gender. To address this question and others, we go a step beyond conventional topic modeling and use structural topic models (STM; Blei & Lafferty, 2007;M. E. Roberts et al., 2014;Wang et al., 2011). STMs account for correlations among topics rather than assuming they are independent (Blei & Lafferty, 2007) and factor in associations with outside covariates (M. E. Roberts et al., 2019). In other words, rather than assuming the topic distribution for a document is independent of another document, STMs help capture possible associations between documents via key covariates like the race or gender of an applicant. Similarly, STMs also allow for dependence between the word distributions for topics captured by the same or a different set of covariates. Thus, STM techniques allow for even more nuanced analyses of the relationships among topics and analyses of the relationships between topics and outside context.

Once we extracted topics from our text corpora (i.e., letters of recommendation and personal statements -note that these corpora were modeled separately), we drew on a method called policy capture (Cooksey, 1996) to analyze how the information in the application package was used to decide to accept or reject each applicant. Traditionally, policy capture has evaluated cue usage using regression-based methods, with variance captured by individual predictors representing the weight those cues had on the decision process (e.g., Dawes, 1971;Spence & Keeping, 2009;Dahling & Thompson, 2010). of these topics has a probability of generating words that appear in the text. From the probability that a topic generated a word, one can calculate the overall probability that said topic produced a given document.

Ordinarily, unstructured data is difficult for regression to accommodate and requires specialized techniques (Putka et al., 2022). This has caused issues when adapting policy capture to more naturalistic contexts, where structured cues are often more difficult to come by (Ebbesen & Konecni, 1980;Shiffrin, 2021). By deriving the structure of our documents through topic modeling, however, we can adapt the latent meaning within them into a quantitative, numerical form (i.e., the topic proportions for each document). This approach allows us to explore the question: when provided both structured and unstructured data, how did decision makers integrate them?

sections.paper_id: Adaryukov et al. - 2025 - Worth the weight An examination of unstructured and structured data in graduate admissions.
paragraphs: We aim to examine the processes graduate admissions committees use to reach a decision from structured and unstructured data. This study offers a unique opportunity to examine high-stakes admissions decisions in the real world and, through natural language processing and Bayesian regression, estimate the decision policies by which students were admitted or rejected.

Often, the attributes that are considered when reviewing applications are quantitative. However, unstructured/qualitative data are key to many admissions decisions. To understand how these decisions are made, we must first understand what information this unstructured data conveys to decision-makers. Research and theory suggest this process may differ based on the applicant's race and gender. Therefore, our first question is as follows:
Q1a: What cues are most prevalent in unstructured application documents?
Additionally, Q1b: Do the interpretations and weights of those cues vary by applicant race and gender?

To answer this question, we performed structural topic modeling (M. E. Roberts et al., 2014;M. E. Roberts, Stewart, & Airoldi, 2016;M. C. Roberts & Ostreko, 2018; M. E. Roberts et al., 2019) on student recommendation letters and personal statements.

To establish the degree to which race and gender were associated with the recommendation letter and personal statement topics, we took a two-step approach. First, we conducted a model comparison between a set of topic models that included or excluded the demographic information as covariates (in terms of held-out likelihood fit and semantic coherence). Second, we estimated the correlations between applicant demographic variables and the estimated topic distributions from the best-fitting topic model.

Admissions decisions involve trading off the relative values of the component attributes to select the objects (i.e., applicants) whose attributes are the most desirable overall; however, the trade-offs among these attributes become unclear when framed in different terms. Therefore, our second question is as follows:
Q2: Of the structured and unstructured decision cues within a graduate admissions process, which were most associated with admission decisions?

To address this question, we also took a two-step approach. First, we compared the predictive performance of sets of models that contained different combinations of quantitative, qualitative, and demographic variables. This comparison helped establish which sets of variables best-predicted admissions decisions. Second, we examined the regression coefficients from the full regression model that contained all of the information from the available admission material to evaluate the degree to which individual cues uniquely contributed to predicting the admission decision.

sections.paper_id: Adaryukov et al. - 2025 - Worth the weight An examination of unstructured and structured data in graduate admissions.
Adaryukov et al. - 2025 - Worth the weight An examination of unstructured and structured data in graduate admissions.
paragraphs: We analyzed graduate applications to the University of Kansas, collected from the Fall 2012-Spring 2013 application cycle through the university's application system (ADMIT) (n = 2, 231). Applications came from 37 different departments throughout the university, including fields in STEM, the social sciences, and the humanities. Only departments whose Chairs and Directors permitted their applicants' deidentified data to be included were used. For each applicant, the data set contained whether the student was admitted or denied admittance to their chosen program. The number of applications to each department varied wildly, with a mean of 60.3 and a standard deviation of 57.1. The department with the most applications in the sample was Psychology, with 232 applicants (41 admitted, 191 denied). The department with the fewest applications was Music Education and Music Therapy, with a single applicant (denied). Lacking an a priori reason to exclude any particular department, we included all departments in the analysis. We accounted for departmental differences via our hierarchical regressions, treating the department as a higher-order group. One might argue that smaller departments may not have a strong selection process and, therefore, may not weigh any particular attribute from the application materials. We chose to let the data analysis inform us of this.

All data were fully anonymized with respect to applicant names, addresses, prior universities, letter writer names, and professors of interest. Students seeking master's (n = 968) and doctoral (n = 1, 263) degrees were included in the decision models. 2 The distribution of each department's number of applicants and acceptance rate is depicted in Figure 2.
Structured data. The structured data consisted of numeric variables describing each applicant's grades and test scores and categorical variables describing each applicant's demographics.

Numeric predictors. The primary quantitative variables available were GPA and GRE scores for individual test categories. GPA was reported on the U.S. standard scale of 1 to 4 and subdivided into three categories: undergraduate GPA, graduate GPA, and highest GPA of the two. The highest GPA available was used to ensure that candidates with and without prior graduate degrees could be compared. Furthermore, international GPAs were converted to United States equivalents using the Scholaro GPA Calculator.foot_1

The average GPA of applicants was 3.47 (SD = 0.37). GRE scores were subdivided into Verbal, Quantitative, and Analytical sections. To ensure consistency of scales, GRE scores in Verbal and Quantitative sections were remapped to the current 170-point scale using equivalent scores from the admission year before the analysis and then standardized using z-scoring; GRE scores in the Analytical section were remapped to the current 6-point scale before standardization. Average scores prior to standardization were 152.40 (SD = 14.48) for Verbal, 153.97 (SD = 11.02) for Quantitative, and 3.75 (SD = 0.85) for Analytical.

Demographic predictors. Demographic data on gender, race, and degree sought were included in each application. Gender was coded as a binary of either Female or Male; no neutral option was given at the time of collection. Of the 1,925 applicants who reported their gender (i.e., 86% of the total sample), 876 (45%) reported being female and 1,047 (55%) reported being male.

Race was also coded categorically. Of the 1,151 domestic applicants who reported their race (i.e., 51.6% of the total sample), 1,048 (91%) reported being White, 51 (4.4%) reported being Asian, and 47 (4.1%) reported being Black. The remaining 9 (0.8%) participants were grouped into an "Other" category for this analysis. Multiracial applicants were coded based on their first reported race. Ethnicity was coded separately from race, with 45 applicants (3.9%) reporting being Hispanic or Latino. Finally, 1,034 applicants (46%) were international applicants and, as a result, had no racial classification in the ADMIT system. Our analyses treated these international applicants as belonging to a separate racial category. The majority of applicants were either domestic White or International, with very few applicants reporting any other race; this makes drawing conclusions about those groups difficult. Therefore, our analyses will focus primarily on domestic White vs. International applicants.
Unstructured data. The unstructured data consisted of the following documents: one personal statement and an average of 2.703 recommendation letters (SD = 0.596).

Personal statements averaged 798 words in length (SD = 334), while recommendation letters averaged 342 words (SD = 190). The word count and squared word count of each document were included as cues in the decision model to allow for the possibility of a parabolic relationship (e.g., raters preferring documents of intermediate length).

Separate topic models were created for letters and personal statements (see "Structural Topic Modeling" below). Letter and statement corpora were processed using the tidytext R package (Silge & Robinson, 2016), such that stop words, punctuation, words with character lengths two or more standard deviations above average (calculated after stop word removal), and numbers were removed. For analyses, individual words were used as tokens. (Code for the entire processing pipeline is provided in the supplemental materials.) The propensity of each document to contain each identified/derived topic was extracted from each topic model and then analyzed using hierarchical Bayesian models (described below).

sections.paper_id: Adaryukov et al. - 2025 - Worth the weight An examination of unstructured and structured data in graduate admissions.
paragraphs: Structural Topic Modeling. To obtain a representation of the latent information within applicants' unstructured models, STMs were used (M. E. Roberts et al., 2014).

STMs are extensions of Correlated Topic Models (CTMs), and like them, allow topics within a corpus to be correlated (Blei & Lafferty, 2007). For instance, if letters that heavily feature a topic on classwork also frequently feature a topic on writing skills, STMs can model this correlation in their distributions of topics, rather than holding all topics as mutually independent. This allows for a more nuanced understanding of topic relationships. Furthermore, STMs extend this property by allowing topics to correlate with covariates of the documents, such as the applicant's intended department and gender. If a topic about stem cells is more common among biology department students, for instance, the STM can account for this, too.

Like other probabilistic topic models, structural topic models are data-driven and probabilistic. They are data-driven in the sense that the topics are derived from the corpus rather than assigned a priori. Additionally, they are probabilistic in the sense that topics are modeled as probability distributions over words and documents are modeled as probability distributions over topics. In other words, a topic's content is evaluated based on the probability that certain words are generated by it, and its prevalence is evaluated by the probability that it is contained within certain documents.

As with other probabilistic topic models, STMs assume that each document in a corpus C was generated via a Bayesian process. For each document d, a distribution of topics Θ d is drawn from a multinomial distribution centered around a global prior. For each topic within that distribution, a distribution of word probabilities β is drawn, centered around a separate global prior. In turn, for each word, a topic assignment z j is drawn from Θ d . That is, z wj ∼ Multinom(Θ d ). Each word is assumed to have been generated with a probability conditioned on the topic assignment of that word and its distribution of probabilities, p(w j |z j , β). Based on this generative model, a topic model uses Bayes' rule and statistical inference to infer the underlying distributions of topics and words based on the observed words within documents.

Starting from the observed word counts and a set of global priors assumed by the model, a topic model uses matrices of word-by-topic counts to evaluate the joint probability distribution of words and topics, P (w, z). Knowing these probabilities, one can derive the probability of a topic given a specific document using Bayes' rule: P (z|w) = P (w|z)P (z) P (w) (Griffiths et al., 2007). Because the marginal probability P (w) in the denominator is computationally intractable, topic model parameter values are estimated algorithmically. STM uses expectation maximization to estimate this value (M. E. Roberts, Stewart, & Airoldi, 2016).

A topic model yields two variables of interest: the probability of each topic per document, henceforth θ, and the probability of each word per topic, henceforth β. The parameter θ measures topic prevalence, representing how prevalent a topic is likely to be within a document. The parameter β can be used to discern topic content, as words with higher probabilities of being generated by a topic are more likely to represent that topic's meaning. For most probabilistic topic models, these variables are derived entirely from the text of the documents. However, STMs make both of these variables conditional on document covariates, such as the race and gender of the applicant. These covariates are represented as separate matrices of information, X for prevalence and Y for content, and can include discrete and continuous variables. By incorporating these covariates, STMs can account for differences in how often different groups incorporate certain topics within their documents or how they talk about the same topic. Without content covariates, this function simplifies to a correlated topic model.

Implementation. We implemented STM with the stm R package (M. E. Roberts et al., 2019). Models were initialized using latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA) matrices, which provide a good model fit for relatively low numbers of documents at very low computational costs (M. E. Roberts, Stewart, & Tingley, 2016).

Our first step in using an STM was to answer two main questions: how many topics to estimate, and whether to model applicant race, gender, and department as possible covariates in the STM. We took a model comparison approach to make these decisions. We summarize the results of the model comparison approach in Supplementary S1. Regarding the number of topics, we used cross-validation to compare STM models with a different number of topics ranging from 1 to 50. The fit statistics indicated diminishing improvements past 19 topics for recommendation letters and personal statements.
Therefore, we set the number of topics to 19 for both the STMs of the personal statements and letters of recommendation.

Regarding the type and number of covariates, we compared models with and without demographic covariates (of topic prevalence and content) in a held-out data set. We held out half of the text from 10% of our documents, trained our topic models on the remaining words in each corpus, and then fit the resulting models to our heldout data to evaluate their predictive performance. We first examined the distributions of two performance measures: the held-out likelihoods and semantic coherence (see Supplementary S1 for an explanation of these measures). Results indicated very similar fit across models. Therefore, we examined other characteristics of model performance, including interpretability and sparsity of topics, to make a final decision. The final model we selected included race and gender as prevalence covariates only.

A topic model with the "best" number of topics and covariates is not necessarily the most interpretable (Chang et al., 2009). As the questions of this study specifically involved how text interpretation correlates to decision-making, it was essential to consider the topics produced by the topic models through a lens that could conceivably approximate that of an application reader. Typically, a topic's content is represented by the words it has the highest probability of generating, i.e., by β. However, β will weight words with a higher probability of occurring throughout the corpus more highly, regardless of whether these words are specific to that topic. To resolve this issue, we instead used words with the highest Frequency-Exclusivity (FrEx; Bischof & Airoldi, 2012, see Supplementary S2.1 for a more detailed explanation). Our interpretation of the semantic content of each topic was thus guided by the 40 words with the highest FrEx in each corpus. Descriptive labels for each topic were manually created and cross-referenced by three members of our research team. Each team member viewed the highest FrEx words per topic and, when needed, the contents of the document where that topic was most prevalent. Based on this information, each team member created their own name for the topic. Names were then sent around and voted upon by our team members to determine which name was the most appropriate per topic.

sections.header_raw: Policy capture analysis. Decision analysis was performed using hierarchical
sections.header_norm: policy capture analysis decision analysis was performed using hierarchical
paragraphs: Bayesian modeling using the brms R package (Bürkner, 2017). The binary outcome variable was the final admission decision, i.e., the department's decision to admit or deny each applicant. The predictor variables were the cues described below (i.e., topic prevalence scores, GPA, and GRE scores). All predictor variables were standardized before entry into the regression model, allowing effect sizes and credible intervals to be compared meaningfully across all categories of information. Model estimation was accomplished using Markov Chain Monte Carlo using the No-U-Turn Sampler algorithm (Hoffman & Gelman, 2014). Models were run for four chains, each with 1000 warm-up iterations and 7000 inference iterations. Due to the limitations of logistic regression, participants with missing data were excluded from the analyses; a total of n = 1, 722 applicants were included in these regressions. A total of n = 392 applicants lacked GRE scores (all three were missing), As different departments in our sample were likely to follow different decision rules, we modeled the variability across departments by allowing each department to have a random (or varying) intercept and a random (or varying) slope for each cue (Bürkner, 2018). This structure permitted us to model department-level variability both in baseline acceptance rates and in cue weights. Initially, we attempted to include 51 cues in the full regression model. However, this led to model estimation problems and goes against standard practice in policy capture research, which suggests using few cues per model (Stewart, 1988). Instead, topics were coded by the first author and two research assistants as either department-specific (e.g., topic 1 from the personal statements about military history, international policy, and Eastern Europe) or department-agnostic (e.g., topic 2 from the personal statements about reasons for applying to the University of Kansas).

Only department-agnostic topics were included in the regression model, resulting in twenty-three distinct cues (detailed below). Random slopes in this analysis used Gaussian priors with a mean of 0 and a standard deviation of 1,000 on the population-level effects, to allow for a structured but weakly informative prior. 4 The race/ethnicity/international variable was dummy coded with White as the reference group, and the gender variable was dummy coded with Female as the reference group.

A key question we were interested in was the degree to which different types of structured and unstructured data contributed to predicting admission decisions. One way to answer this question is with model comparison. A set of models was created based on the three unique categories of predictors within our data-qualitative (topics from letters and statements), quantitative (GPA and GRE), and demographic-as well as all possible combinations of those categories. Each model was compared using leave-one-out cross-validation information criteria, or LOOIC (Vehtari et al., 2017). The LOOIC quantifies how well a model can predict the likelihood of a held-out data point after being fit to the other data. In addition, we constructed confusion matrices for each model to gauge sensitivity and specificity-in this case, the ability of each model to accurately predict admittance and denial, respectively. Finally, we compared the posterior distributions of model predictions to determine whether the predicted probability of acceptance for accepted students was credibly different from that for rejected students.

sections.header_raw: Structural Topic Modeling of Admissions Documents
sections.header_norm: structural topic modeling of admissions documents
paragraphs: The first step of our analysis was to assess how to model the topics within our admissions documents.
To determine what topic model to use, we performed a two-stage model comparison of various topic models with different prevalence and content covariates.

This comparison yielded the demographic model with 19 topics as our best fit model. In this model, applicant race and gender were associated with topic prevalence or how often topics appeared in documents. For more details on the model comparison process, see Supplementary S1. Note that letters and statements were considered separately in topic modeling, so each document type had its own corpus.

Once the appropriate topic model had been established, our next step was to analyze and interpret the topics generated by that model. The first author interpreted and labeled topics based on the ten highest-FrEx words they contained, then had two additional members of the research team look at the same information and come up with their own topic names. These names were then voted on by the three members of the research team to determine which would be most appropriate for the given topic. In addition, topics were coded as department-specific or department-agnostic based on whether their highest-FrEx words related to a single field of study, as determined by the research team. Supplementary S3 shows the labels for the topics identified for analysis, along with the ten highest-FrEx words for each of them. Figure 3 shows word clouds for specific topics of interest, with topic interpretations. For distribution of topics within documents, see Supplementary S6.

The content of topics produced by STM varied considerably, with 11 (58%) of the letter of recommendation topics and 14 (74%) of the personal statement topics being coded as department-specific. Many department-agnostic topics were related to different dimensions of praise, such as hyperbolic phrasing and general endorsement. However, there were differences between corpora on what department-agnostic topics appeared; those in the Letters tended to refer to personal qualities or general background (i.e., Aspirations and Preparedness, Favorable Appreciation, Intuition and Curiosity), while the personal statements yielded more topics focused on specific research (i.e., Research Output, Research Dreams). Attempting to control for the department-specific topics by including department as a content covariate in our topic models resulted in overly-sparse topics and worse model fit. The implication of the high amount of department-specific topics is that many applicants focused their documents on explaining their specific work or research interests to their chosen department. This may be a natural consequence of unstructured qualitative data: while it can convey large amounts of unique information, it might be primarily geared at specific individuals and difficult to evaluate or generalize outside of that context.

The implication of the high amount of department-specific topics is that many applicants focused their documents on explaining their specific work or research interests to their chosen department. This may be a natural consequence of unstructured qualitative data: while it can convey large amounts of unique information, it might be primarily geared at specific individuals and difficult to evaluate or generalize outside of that context.

The first step of our analysis was to assess how to model the topics within our admissions documents.

To determine what topic model to use, we performed a two-stage model comparison of various topic models with different prevalence and content covariates.

This comparison yielded the demographic model with 19 topics as our best fit model. In this model, applicant race and gender were associated with topic prevalence or how often topics appeared in documents. For more details on the model comparison process, see Supplementary S1. Note that letters and statements were considered separately in topic modeling, so each document type had its own corpus.

Once the appropriate topic model had been established, our next step was to analyze and interpret the topics generated by that model. The first author interpreted and labeled topics based on the ten highest-FrEx words they contained, then had two additional members of the research team look at the same information and come up with their own topic names. These names were then voted on by the three members of the research team to determine which would be most appropriate for the given topic. In addition, topics were coded as department-specific or department-agnostic based on whether their highest-FrEx words related to a single field of study, as determined by the research team. Supplementary S3 shows the labels for the topics identified for analysis, along with the ten highest-FrEx words for each of them. Figure 3 shows word clouds for specific topics of interest, with topic interpretations. For distribution of topics within documents, see Supplementary S6.

The content of topics produced by STM varied considerably, with 11 (58%) of the letter of recommendation topics and 14 (74%) of the personal statement topics being coded as department-specific. Many department-agnostic topics were related to different dimensions of praise, such as hyperbolic phrasing and general endorsement. sections.header_raw: How Do Cues Correlate With Applicant Demographics?
sections.header_norm: how do cues correlate with applicant demographics
paragraphs: As mentioned, the topic models incorporated race and gender as factors associated with topic prevalence. Our next step was to examine the strength of these associations. As an initial check, we used the estimateEffect() function in the stm R package to look at these associations. None of the topic coefficients for race and gender were credibly different from zero, suggesting that there was little relationship between these demographic variables and topics once common variation was accounted for simultaneously. This was true for topics from both recommendation letters and personal statements. For a second check, we also calculated zero-order correlations between race and gender and the topic prevalence parameters θ to establish the empirical baseline association between them. To clarify our results, we examined the mean prevalence of each topic per demographic. Recommendation Letter Topics. Figure 5 shows the zero-order correlations between recommendation letter topics, applicant race and gender, and the likelihood of admission. The strongest correlations for letter topics occurred for White and International students. In particular, Favorable Appreciation and Aspirations and Preparedness topics were positively correlated with International Status and negatively correlated with identifying as White. Analogously, Hyperbolic and General Endorsement were negatively correlated with International Status and positively correlated with identifying as White.

The correlations with race were coded such that the focal race was with all other groups. Thus, we examined the mean topic prevalence levels for each race. Doing so showed that the correlations were largely driven by different topic prevalences for International Status, as seen in Figure 4. Specifically, International applicants' letter-writers had higher propensities for Favorable Appreciation and Aspirations and Preparedness while lower propensities for Hyperbolic and General Endorsement. As a reminder, White and International constituted the overwhelming majority of the sample, making it difficult to draw conclusions about any other racial group. 5

Personal Statement Topics. The broad pattern for recommendation letter topics was also true for personal statement topics, as shown in Figure 6. Topics strongly correlated with International status were strongly correlated in the opposite direction with identifying as White. The three topics that stood out were Why KU, Research Dreams, University Honors. Examining mean topic prevalence levels indicated that these correlations were driven by international applicants showing low prevalence levels for Why KU, but high levels for Research Dreams and University Honors.

Quantitative Cues. While topics were modeled to account for demographic associations, our quantitative cues were not. However, a similar trend of demographic associations exists for GRE and GPA scores in our sample, as shown in Figure 7, such that cues positively correlated with identifying as White also negatively correlated with identifying as International. Notably, international status correlated strongly with higher quantitative GRE scores (r = 0.45 [0.41, 0.49]), while identifying as white correlated strongly with higher verbal GRE scores (r = 0.37 [0.33, 0.41]). However, demographics did not strongly correlate with acceptance one way or the other (see Figure 7).

paragraphs: Accepted Mean [95% CI] Rejected Mean [95% CI] Full Model 0.0 [0.0] 0.673 [0.648, 0.698] 0.801 [0.783, 0.818] Qualitative and Quantitative -0.8 [4.8] 0.662 [0.636, 0.687] 0.794 [0.776, 0.812] Quantitative and Demographic -4.4 [9.5] 0.635 [0.612, 0.660] 0.778 [0.760, 0.796] Quantitative Only -22.5 [11.9] 0.614 [0.588, 0.637] 0.764 [0.745, 0.783] Qualitative and Demographic -84.6 [14.3] 0.614 [0.537, 0.640] 0.765 [0.746, 0.783] Qualitative Only -86.5 [14.8] 0.600 [0.575, 0.627] 0.756 [0.738, 0.775] Demographic Only -114.7 [17.6] 0.560 [0.535, 0.586] 0.732 [0.712, 0.751] Note. Estimated Least Squares difference from the best fit after Leave-One Out (LOO) cross-validation, and predicted mean and HDI correct prediction for accepted and rejected students are shown here. The means and 95% highest density intervals of each distribution are reported. Accepted means are the model's estimated mean probability of acceptance for accepted students, while rejected means are the model's estimated mean probability of rejection for rejected students. The higher these numbers, the more accurate the model is at classifying accepted and rejected students, respectively.

The full model, which included qualitative, quantitative, and demographic information, provided the best fit across all participants. The qualitative and quantitative models, as well as the quantitative and demographic models, performed reasonably well in terms of prediction; the fit of subsequent models dropped precipitously. Essentially, models without quantitative data struggled to accurately predict admissions decisions, indicating that quantitative data had a particularly high relevance. However, quantitative data also predicted significantly worse alone than in conjunction with other data. For instance, the qualitative and quantitative model could identify admitted students with 66.2% sensitivity, while the model with only quantitative cues could only identify 61.4% of admitted students. It is also worth noting that specificity-the ability to detect denied students correctly-was 13% greater than the sensitivity in the full model, indicating that they tended to overpredict denial rather than admission.

In addition to these predictions, we also estimated model accuracy. To do so, we calculated the probability of a correct decision. This probability is the weighted average of the probability of accepting an applicant given they were accepted and the probability of rejecting an applicant given they were rejected weighted by the proportion of accepted and rejected applicants (respectively). Supplementary Table S6 shows a table of these comparisons. The full model was the most accurate, with a mean accuracy of 0.75 [0.74, 0.76], while the model with only demographic covariates was the weakest, with a mean accuracy of 0.66 [0.65, 0.67]. Models with quantitative cues performed better than those without, although differences in accuracy from model to model were generally within .02. This could indicate that quantitative cues were doing heavy predictive lifting across departments.

To ensure we were predicting above base rates, we compared the predictions of acceptance and rejection at the department level, as well as accuracy at the department level, to base rates of admission. Supplementary Table S7 summarizes these comparisons.

While a few departments' accuracy was at or near chance level, most departments predicted well above chance. Additionally, most departments' projected probabilities of acceptance for accepted students were well above that department's base rate, reinforcing the fact that the models were performing well at the department level.

sections.paper_id: Adaryukov et al. - 2025 - Worth the weight An examination of unstructured and structured data in graduate admissions.
sections.header_raw: How Did Admissions Decision-Makers Weight The Cues They Chose?
sections.header_norm: how did admissions decision makers weight the cues they chose
paragraphs: For the next step in our analysis, we used the full decision model with quantitative, qualitative, and demographic data to evaluate how the different individual cues were associated with the admissions decision. Supplementary Figure S8 shows the

population-level coefficient values for each predictor in the full model (see Supplementary S7 for coefficients in table form). At the population level, seven cues in the full model had credible associations with admissions across departments. GPA (β = 0.64 [0.34, 0.95]), verbal GRE (β = 0.61 [0.36, 0.89]), analytical GRE (β = 0.26 [0.03, 0.51]), quantitative GRE (β = 0.40 [0.15, 0.65]), and recommendation letter word count (β = 0.32 [0.06, 0.59]) were credibly associated with admission. Meanwhile, the Favorable Appreciation recommendation letter topic (β = -0.39 [-0.75, -0.04]) and the Perseverance recommendation letter topic (β = -0.28 [-0.52, -0.04]) were credibly associated with denial at this level. Qualitative cues universally had wider credible intervals than

quantitative cues, indicating that the estimations of topic cue weights had higher variability. Furthermore, the fact that the only credible topics from the personal statements and the letters of recommendation were those that predicted denial suggests that qualitative materials, particularly the recommendation letters, were used primarily to check for reasons not to admit an applicant. However, these effects were not consistent within each department.
Figure 8 shows the estimated coefficients for each cue at the department level.

Overall, cues tended to have stable estimates across departments, even those with smaller student populations. Most cues' credible intervals were not different from 0. However, some cues had a strong presence across departments; for instance, GRE Verbal had positive credible associations with admission in thirteen departments, while GPA had positive credible associations with admission in ten departments. While there was some variability in how departments used GPA, its point estimates were consistently positive, even when the credible interval around them included 0. Department estimates of qualitative cue weights also showed wider credible intervals than estimates of quantitative cue weights, reflecting the trends seen at the population level. Findings of this nature are to be expected, as different departments can reasonably be assumed to have different admissions criteria. Additionally, some departments have far fewer students than others, so some of the variance might be attributed to lack of power in those particular departments.

However, the relative dearth of departments in which credible associations were found is still striking. Compared to coefficients for GRE and GPA, coefficients for topics were more universally consistent across departments, even though they were not credible. Given how few departments seemed to rely on the topics extracted from the personal statements and recommendation letters, no general claims can be made about reasons to deny admission. around an effect includes 0, the effect is not credible. Credible intervals across departments can be found at the bottom of each panel. Note that coefficients for GRE Verbal, GRE Analytical, GRE Quantitative, and GPA vary considerably by department, in accordance with expectations (i.e. higher weighting of Verbal in history and sociology, higher weighting of Quantitative in physics and pharmacology).

It is worth noting that this graduate application dataset contained applications for Ph.D. and Master's programs at the University of Kansas. These two types of programs differ in myriad ways, including the fact that students admitted for a Ph.D. often receive a stipend and tuition remission. In contrast, students admitted to a Master's program typically pay tuition. This raises the question of whether admissions materials may have been used differently for these two types of applications. We reran the full model by adding a Ph.D indicator variable to address this question. We also included interactions between the Ph.D variable and cues which were credible in the original full model. Overall, the results of the model were much the same as those in the model discussed above. However, the Favorable Appreciation topic was no longer credibly associated with admissions decisions, and there was a credible interaction between word count and Ph.D-seeking status. No other interactions were credible, nor was Ph.D-seeking status on its own.
Coefficients for this model can be found in Supplementary S8.

Furthermore, the fact that the only credible topics from the personal statements and the letters of recommendation were those that predicted denial suggests that qualitative materials, particularly the recommendation letters, were used primarily to check for reasons not to admit an applicant. However, these effects were not consistent within each department.

Figure 8 shows the estimated coefficients for each cue at the department level.

Overall, cues tended to have stable estimates across departments, even those with smaller student populations. Most cues' credible intervals were not different from 0. However, some cues had a strong presence across departments; for instance, GRE Verbal had positive credible associations with admission in thirteen departments, while GPA had positive credible associations with admission in ten departments. While there was some variability in how departments used GPA, its point estimates were consistently positive, even when the credible interval around them included 0. Department estimates of qualitative cue weights also showed wider credible intervals than estimates of quantitative cue weights, reflecting the trends seen at the population level. Findings of this nature are to be expected, as different departments can reasonably be assumed to have different admissions criteria. Additionally, some departments have far fewer students than others, so some of the variance might be attributed to lack of power in those particular departments.

However, the relative dearth of departments in which credible associations were found is still striking. Compared to coefficients for GRE and GPA, coefficients for topics were more universally consistent across departments, even though they were not credible. Given how few departments seemed to rely on the topics extracted from the personal statements and recommendation letters, no general claims can be made about reasons to deny admission. around an effect includes 0, the effect is not credible. Credible intervals across departments can be found at the bottom of each panel. Note that coefficients for GRE Verbal, GRE Analytical, GRE Quantitative, and GPA vary considerably by department, in accordance with expectations (i.e. higher weighting of Verbal in history and sociology, higher weighting of Quantitative in physics and pharmacology).

It is worth noting that this graduate application dataset contained applications for Ph.D. and Master's programs at the University of Kansas. These two types of programs differ in myriad ways, including the fact that students admitted for a Ph.D. often receive a stipend and tuition remission. In contrast, students admitted to a Master's program typically pay tuition. This raises the question of whether admissions materials may have been used differently for these two types of applications. We reran the full model by adding a Ph.D indicator variable to address this question. We also included interactions between the Ph.D variable and cues which were credible in the original full model. Overall, the results of the model were much the same as those in the model discussed above. However, the Favorable Appreciation topic was no longer credibly associated with admissions decisions, and there was a credible interaction between word count and Ph.D-seeking status. No other interactions were credible, nor was Ph.D-seeking status on its own.

Coefficients for this model can be found in Supplementary S8.

sections.paper_id: Adaryukov et al. - 2025 - Worth the weight An examination of unstructured and structured data in graduate admissions.
sections.header_raw: How Reliable Is Our Unstructured Data?
sections.header_norm: how reliable is our unstructured data
paragraphs: One potential issue with unstructured data is that of reliability. Compared to structured and widely-used variables such as GRE with test-retest reliability of .84 (Lee & Wai, 2023) and split-half reliability of .92, topics derived from our documents are liable to have higher measurement error. That is, their results are highly susceptible to the exact words within a corpus. This could affect the regression weights for topics in general, and may explain why topics largely did not register in the decision to admit.

To examine the reliability of our topics, we developed a method to estimate the split-half reliability. To do so, we estimated 100 split-half correlations each for letters and statements. For each corpus we removed half the words randomly from every document.

We then fit our topic model to each half of the words. Next we took the resulting estimates of the topic prevalence θ from each half and correlated them with each other. We repeated this 100 times with 100 new random splits. Finally, we performed the Spearman-Brown correction on all split-half correlations to obtain an estimate of the reliabilities; the results of this correction are reported below.

We report the summaries of the correlations in the Supplementary Material (Table S8 and Table S9). The split-half reliabilities for topics taken from recommendation letters averaged 0.246 (SD = 0.062), while the split-half reliabilities for topics taken from personal statements averaged 0.431 (SD = 0.077). Topics from personal statements had a wider range of reliability than topics taken from recommendation letters, with the University Honors topic having the highest split-half reliability of M = 0.700 (SD = 0.037), and the Research Dreams topic having the lowest split-half reliability M = 0.363 (SD = 0.087).

Taken together, these results indicate that topics had much lower reliability than their structured data counterparts. 6 This lower reliability raises additional questions and limitations regarding our policy capture analysis, especially in comparing the predictive validity of structured and unstructured data when the latter has much lower reliability.

Our initial conclusion was that the largely non-credible and low regression weights for the topics indicate that decision makers were largely not using the letters or personal statements (and hence the topics) in making a decision. However, one possibility is that lower reliability in the topic explains the lower regression weights. Another possibility is that decision makers are aware of the lower reliability and thus weigh the topics lower in their decisions because their reliability is low.

To try and address these alternative explanations, we took advantage of the 6 Additionally, as a check of the robustness of our results, we performed a second analysis of the letters of recommendation using BERTopic (Grootendorst, 2022). BERTopic is a recent evolution of topic modeling;

because of features such as its use of word embedding to generate topics and its use of an existing large-language model as a pre-training corpus, its topics are more consistent and reliable than those of LDA and its offshoots. BERTopic yielded three unique topics for letters of recommendation and fourteen for personal statements, fewer than the suggested number in STM. These topics were added to a regression analysis alongside quantitative cues; in this regression, no topics were credibly associated with admissions decisions in either direction. Code and additional details for this analysis can be found on OSF.

variability in the split-half correlations across the topics and asked whether for a given topic if the split-half correlation we estimated was related to the magnitude of its regression coefficient in the full model. If decision makers were sensitive to these different levels of reliabilities, then there should be a positive relationship. Similarly, if the level of reliability was impacting the estimate of the regression estimate there would also be a positive relationship. However, we found no evidence for such a relationship, with a negligible correlation between reliability and regression coefficients (r = 0.03 [-0.42, 0.47]).

If anything, the opposite was true; when correlating reliabilities with absolute values of regression coefficients (a means of checking for strength of coefficient alone), evidence of a negative relationship emerged (r = -0.25 [-0.63, 0.25]). While we cannot rule any alternative explanation out, this result seems to support our original conclusion that decision makers are not allocating a lot of weight to the unstructured documents when making admissions decisions. 7

sections.paper_id: Adaryukov et al. - 2025 - Worth the weight An examination of unstructured and structured data in graduate admissions.
paragraphs: Our results suggest that admissions committees relied more consistently on structured data in their decisions to admit applicants to graduate programs than on unstructured data. We first identified the contents of the unstructured data through structural topic modeling. Many of the topics identified by STM in the recommendation letters and personal statements were department-specific, although some topics with more general meanings did emerge. The prevalence of some topics did correlate with demographic characteristics such as international status and gender, and incorporating these as covariates improved the fit of the STM for both recommendation letters and 7 We also examined the relationship between topic reliabilities and the standard deviation of the posterior distribution of the regression coefficients. Lower reliability should also lead to greater uncertainty in the value of the regression coefficients. We did find evidence of a negative relationship between reliabilities and the standard deviation, albeit not a credible one (r = -0.24 [-0.62, 0.23]). This shows that the analysis was sensitive to reliability differences in our topics.
personal statements. We then modeled the admissions decisions made by each department using topics, scores, and applicant demographics.

While individual cue utilization varied by department, structured quantitative information had the most consistent credible associations with the decision to admit, particularly GPA. Incorporating qualitative cues from the unstructured data improved model predictions, but not by much. This could indicate that this information did not weigh into the decision process or provided redundant information with applicants' quantitative data. 8 Model accuracy was reasonable (75%; see Table S6), but still had room for improvement. We suspect one aspect impacting model performance is that we did not have curricula vitae (CVs) or interview information available to model. Furthermore, demographics did not have independent associations with admissions after topics were controlled for. Essentially, the data support the idea that admissions decisions during the time frame examined were driven first by grades and test scores. In other words, admissions decisions were based primarily on structured data.

One reason decision makers might have weighted structured data more heavily is that most information within recommendation letters was department-specific discussion. Out of nineteen topics identified in recommendation letters, eleven were directly related to research interests or study areas, such as physics, education, and economics. This proportion was even higher in personal statements, with fourteen of nineteen being department-specific topics. This finding was not entirely unexpected, as both letters and statements ask why applicants would be a good fit for their chosen program, which includes research interests. However, this makes the unique information within those documents very hard to generalize outside of the applicant's department. Max et al. (2010) identified similar issues in the personal statements of applicants for anesthesiology 8 We ran correlations among topics and the quantitative variables. Many topics correlated with GRE and GPA independently, but there was no distinguishing pattern between those credibly associated with admission and those that did not. These correlations can be found in the Supplemental Materials.

residents, with a small subset of identified features, such as enjoyment of hands-on specialties and love of physiology, accounting for the bulk of information across applicants' materials. Not only does this make applicants within a department more homogenized, but it also obscures unique information about that applicant, making documents less useful in identifying potentially successful students. This may be a side-effect of unstructured data, wherein the characteristics that admissions committees seek are "lost in the shuffle."

Of the letter and statement topics that were not department-specific, those most correlated with admissions decisions also varied demographically, particularly for international applicants. International applicants, for example, were less likely to talk about why they wanted to attend KU specifically in their personal statements, particularly relative to White applicants. They were also more likely to highlight honors received at or by their previous university. Recommendation letters for international applicants were also credibly shorter than those for White domestic applicants. Applicants from other countries (and letter writers for those applicants, who are much more likely to be international as well) may be less familiar with unspoken norms regarding style and content of recommendations and personal statements than majority group members (i.e., White applicants and their letter writers) within the United States academic system. When expectations for unstructured data (e.g., letters and statements) are left unspoken, this can disadvantage applicants who are not "in the know" (see Jones, 2013, for a similar example in the United Kingdom). Differences between documents from female and male applicants and from non-White domestic applicants and White domestic applicants were not credibly present in these data. However, due to the dearth of domestic applicants who reported as non-White, it is difficult to draw any definitive conclusions regarding race for domestic applicants.

The policy capture analysis indicated that admissions committees evaluating the applications in our sample weighted structured, quantitative cues more heavily than unstructured, qualitative cues. Of the topics in our final policy model, only two-Favorable Appreciation and Perseverance-from the recommendation letters were credibly associated with admissions, and both had a negative relationship with admittance. Due to the assumed positive valence of these topics, this finding was quite surprising. Examining the five documents with the highest prevalence (θ) values for those topics revealed that lurking variables might have been at play. Documents highest in the Favorable Appreciation topic were all written for international students and devoted extensive space to their English proficiency, and documents highest in Perseverance tended to highlight at least one major weakness in their applicants, such as "he does not concentrate enough on things he doesn't like." As noted earlier, it may be the case that qualitative material was used as an adversarial check after a candidate had cleared a quantitative threshold-essentially, checking for red flags. Reinforcing this idea, adding qualitative information to quantitative improved the regression models' ability to predict admission status correctly, but regression models with only qualitative information performed quite poorly on their own compared to other models. Topics that correlated with denial were also those that were credibly correlated with international status; what might be seen as "red flags" might instead be cultural differences in how international vs.
White domestic applicants approach their statements. This could have an adverse and disproportionate impact on prospects for international students and merits further examination.

That said, additional results from the policy capture make it unclear whether the statistical bias shown translates to biased admissions decisions. Differences between international and White domestic students were found in GRE scores, with international students having higher quantitative GRE scores and lower verbal and analytic scores than White domestic students. However, while GRE scores were positively associated with admissions, this was true of all three test sections, not just verbal and analytic. Of the topics more prevalent in international students' materials, only two were credibly associated with admissions decisions, and only then in specific departments. None of the interviews.

Furthermore, our corpora's wide variety of unique and department-specific information contained many rare words. Of the 30,474 unique terms in recommendation letters and 30,625 unique terms in statements, 86% appeared fewer than ten times. This makes intuitive sense, as recommendation letters and personal statements address specific research interests. We chose not to remove words based on rarity here to ensure our corpora were not overly sparse. However, future studies could adopt the method used by Putka et al. (2022) for qualitative modeling, evaluating the cue weight of individual words first and generating topics through principal component analysis, rather than traditional probabilistic topic modeling. This would also allow the examination of interactions between demographic criteria and specific words, a la Strunk & Bailey (2015) and Kaatz et al. (2015).

It is also possible that the way people interpret meaning from text is very different from the way that structural topic modeling presents it. STM fails to capture potentially important aspects of statements and recommendation letters, including overall quality of writing, ability to provide specific support for claims, and persuasiveness about the degree of fit to a program. These features of unstructured data may matter more for admissions decisions than the topics identified with our approach. These characteristics are generally more subjective and difficult to ascertain with probabilistic topic modeling, which focuses on lexical aspects of writing. Some machine learning techniques are being developed to gauge technical aspects of writing (e.g., structure, syntax, and discourse), but they have not been examined here.

One aspect of structural topic modeling as we have implemented it is that it collapses across departments to obtain topics, and in so doing, highlights words that are common across or within departments. As consequence, the topic models might miss the unique information that each applicant provides, which can be crucial in reaching an admissions decision. While our focus was looking for commonalities across departments, the modeling approach from this study could be applied to single departments, and each department could receive their own topic model, in order to unpack that unique information more thoroughly.

Furthermore, structural topic modeling and unstructured data generally carry reliability issues. Due to their data-driven nature, there will always be variances in the results of a topic model, even when performed for the same documents. We saw this firsthand in our reliability analysis of STM, where reliabilities were much lower than those for GRE and GPA. This low reliability generally leads to regression attenuation, meaning that the true relationship between our topics and decisions might be understated (Schmitt, 1996). To our knowledge, this issue has not been extensively examined for topic models in this context, and while we have performed analyses to check the robustness of our results here, we cannot rule out the impact of low reliability in our conclusions. Future work in this domain could develop methods that enhance the statistical reliability of the topics extracted from topic modeling estimation.

Additionally, while this study focused on archival data analysis, further research could examine how recommenders use recommendation letters and personal statements more directly. For instance, a controlled experiment could be conducted where raters are given students' qualitative documents and asked to rate their overall impression of said documents, before giving their final recommendation for admission. Raters could be asked to name the dimensions on which they evaluate applicants' documents, and their given ratings on those dimensions could be correlated both with admission and with a more data-driven approach such as topic modeling. This approach would address the limitations of topic modeling while accounting for the possibility that raters have different methods of using documents than topics can portray.
