Of the topics in our final policy model, only two-Favorable Appreciation and Perseverance-from the recommendation letters were credibly associated with admissions, and both had a negative relationship with admittance. Due to the assumed positive valence of these topics, this finding was quite surprising. Examining the five documents with the highest prevalence (θ) values for those topics revealed that lurking variables might have been at play. Documents highest in the Favorable Appreciation topic were all written for international students and devoted extensive space to their English proficiency, and documents highest in Perseverance tended to highlight at least one major weakness in their applicants, such as "he does not concentrate enough on things he doesn't like." As noted earlier, it may be the case that qualitative material was used as an adversarial check after a candidate had cleared a quantitative threshold-essentially, checking for red flags. Reinforcing this idea, adding qualitative information to quantitative improved the regression models' ability to predict admission status correctly, but regression models with only qualitative information performed quite poorly on their own compared to other models. Topics that correlated with denial were also those that were credibly correlated with international status; what might be seen as "red flags" might instead be cultural differences in how international vs.

White domestic applicants approach their statements. This could have an adverse and disproportionate impact on prospects for international students and merits further examination.

That said, additional results from the policy capture make it unclear whether the statistical bias shown translates to biased admissions decisions. Differences between international and White domestic students were found in GRE scores, with international students having higher quantitative GRE scores and lower verbal and analytic scores than White domestic students. However, while GRE scores were positively associated with admissions, this was true of all three test sections, not just verbal and analytic. Of the topics more prevalent in international students' materials, only two were credibly associated with admissions decisions, and only then in specific departments. None of the interviews.

Furthermore, our corpora's wide variety of unique and department-specific information contained many rare words. Of the 30,474 unique terms in recommendation letters and 30,625 unique terms in statements, 86% appeared fewer than ten times. This makes intuitive sense, as recommendation letters and personal statements address specific research interests. We chose not to remove words based on rarity here to ensure our corpora were not overly sparse. However, future studies could adopt the method used by Putka et al. (2022) for qualitative modeling, evaluating the cue weight of individual words first and generating topics through principal component analysis, rather than traditional probabilistic topic modeling. This would also allow the examination of interactions between demographic criteria and specific words, a la Strunk & Bailey (2015) and Kaatz et al. (2015).

It is also possible that the way people interpret meaning from text is very different from the way that structural topic modeling presents it. STM fails to capture potentially important aspects of statements and recommendation letters, including overall quality of writing, ability to provide specific support for claims, and persuasiveness about the degree of fit to a program. These features of unstructured data may matter more for admissions decisions than the topics identified with our approach. These characteristics are generally more subjective and difficult to ascertain with probabilistic topic modeling, which focuses on lexical aspects of writing. Some machine learning techniques are being developed to gauge technical aspects of writing (e.g., structure, syntax, and discourse), but they have not been examined here.

One aspect of structural topic modeling as we have implemented it is that it collapses across departments to obtain topics, and in so doing, highlights words that are common across or within departments. As consequence, the topic models might miss the unique information that each applicant provides, which can be crucial in reaching an admissions decision. While our focus was looking for commonalities across departments, the modeling approach from this study could be applied to single departments, and each department could receive their own topic model, in order to unpack that unique information more thoroughly.

Furthermore, structural topic modeling and unstructured data generally carry reliability issues. Due to their data-driven nature, there will always be variances in the results of a topic model, even when performed for the same documents. We saw this firsthand in our reliability analysis of STM, where reliabilities were much lower than those for GRE and GPA. This low reliability generally leads to regression attenuation, meaning that the true relationship between our topics and decisions might be understated (Schmitt, 1996). To our knowledge, this issue has not been extensively examined for topic models in this context, and while we have performed analyses to check the robustness of our results here, we cannot rule out the impact of low reliability in our conclusions. Future work in this domain could develop methods that enhance the statistical reliability of the topics extracted from topic modeling estimation.

Additionally, while this study focused on archival data analysis, further research could examine how recommenders use recommendation letters and personal statements more directly. For instance, a controlled experiment could be conducted where raters are given students' qualitative documents and asked to rate their overall impression of said documents, before giving their final recommendation for admission. Raters could be asked to name the dimensions on which they evaluate applicants' documents, and their given ratings on those dimensions could be correlated both with admission and with a more data-driven approach such as topic modeling. This approach would address the limitations of topic modeling while accounting for the possibility that raters have different methods of using documents than topics can portray.

sections.paper_id: Adaryukov et al. - 2025 - Worth the weight An examination of unstructured and structured data in graduate admissions.
paragraphs: It is worth noting that the data for this study are a product of their time and place.

The applications within this data set were collected from a Midwestern R1 university in 2012-2013. Not all aspects of the University of Kansas's admissions process will generalize to other universities, even those within the United States, and other graduate institutions will likely have different criteria for admissions. Furthermore, the COVID-19 pandemic has changed the admissions process considerably, with many programs at top universities abandoning GRE requirements altogether (Langin, 2022). While admissions decisions here could largely be predicted by quantitative data, a major source of that quantitative data is no longer relevant. However, this study does provide a framework through which graduate admissions across different times and institutions can be studied.

sections.paper_id: Adaryukov et al. - 2025 - Worth the weight An examination of unstructured and structured data in graduate admissions.
paragraphs: If qualitative information is merely used to check for disqualifications, and most qualitative information goes unused, what are the prospects for a truly holistic admissions process? One solution might be to change how qualitative data is collected. Rather than unstructured statements, admissions committees could use structured statements focused on questions not covered elsewhere. Niessen & Neumann (2022) found higher inter-rater reliability in evaluations of structured personal statements than unstructured ones.

Extending this idea, Schmitt et al. (2009) proposed incorporating standardized biographical data and situational judgment tests as complements or substitutes for current student essays. In a situational judgment test, applicants are presented with realistic scenarios and asked to describe how they would assess them (see Christian et al., 2010, for more information). For example, to expand on a candidate's research experience, applicants could be given a research question and asked how they would address it in their own study. Adopting structured approaches could allow more emphasis on topics that can be lost or overlooked in unstructured statements and make expectations for these documents clearer to applicants outside the United States' academic system. As suggested by De Los Reyes & Uddin (2021) and Woo et al. (2023), universities would also be able to share the different cues they use in admissions, creating a database of metrics that could be compared and correlated with long-term performance.

A simpler alternative to revamping how unstructured data is collected might be to have multiple faculty rate the contents of the admissions documents, as well as materials not included here such as CV, on dimensions such as quality of prose and strength of prior accomplishments. They could then transform GRE and GPA into the same scale and tally the results, providing an actuarial judgment without compromising the nuances of unstructured material. While this approach would not solve every issue with unstructured data, it would provide a more even footing on which they could be used with structured cues.

A follow-up to this work could also tap further into the capabilities of machine learning in multiple ways. As mentioned, more sophisticated qualitative modeling could allow for a finer-grain parsing of unstructured data, including style characteristics and the weights of individual words. Using more sophisticated NLP methods, such as RoBERTa, could also offer more predictive methods in exchange for human interpretability (Liu et al., 2020). Additionally, other models, such as random forests, could be used to approximate raters' decision processes. Pilots for this study experimented with decision tree methods, including random forests (Hayes et al., 2015). However, these models could not adequately parse out base rates of admission per department, and while hierarchical versions exist, they have not been implemented in R.

The fact that quantitative data was weighed most frequently in a naturalistic setting raises questions for future research. Will this pattern persist if qualitative data is presented in a more structured format? Does the order in which cues were processed affect which one weighs more heavily in the final decision? Future studies using this framework could address these questions in other contexts with unstructured data. One obvious extension
